skill.name: routeros-fundamentals
skill.description: RouterOS v7 domain knowledge for AI agents. Use when: working with MikroTik RouterOS, writing RouterOS CLI/script commands, calling RouterOS REST API, debugging why a Linux command fails on RouterOS, or when the user mentions MikroTik, RouterOS, CHR, or /ip /system /interface paths. Scope: RouterOS 7.x (long-term and newer) only — v6 is NOT covered and accuracy for v6 problems will be low.
skill.summary: RouterOS v7 domain knowledge for AI agents managing MikroTik network devices.
skill.author: tikoci

security_audit.summary: Static analyzer flagged 434 patterns but all are false positives on documentation content. The skill contains RouterOS CLI reference material and TypeScript usage examples - no executable code. External command patterns are RouterOS CLI syntax (/system/print), network patterns are documentation examples for REST API usage, and crypto/C2 keywords are version strings (rc=release candidate). Safe for publication with minor documentation risk warnings.

low_findings.title: Documentation Contains System Command Examples
low_findings.description: Skill documentation includes RouterOS CLI command examples that could be misused if users copy commands without understanding context. Commands like /system/reboot and /disk/format are documented for educational purposes.

low_findings.title: Network Configuration Examples with Default Credentials
low_findings.description: Documentation shows RouterOS default authentication pattern (admin with empty password) which is accurate for fresh installations but could normalize insecure practices if users do not change defaults.

content.value_statement: AI agents often fail when configuring RouterOS because they apply Linux assumptions to MikroTik's proprietary system. This skill provides authoritative RouterOS v7 domain knowledge including CLI syntax, REST API patterns, scripting language, and version-specific behaviors.
actual_capabilities: Explain RouterOS v7 CLI syntax differences from Unix shell commands
Generate correct RouterOS REST API requests with proper HTTP verb mapping
Write RouterOS scripting language (.rsc) automation scripts
Parse and compare RouterOS version strings including beta and rc releases
Guide device-mode configuration and feature enablement workflows
Identify which extra packages are needed for specific RouterOS features
limitations: RouterOS v6 is NOT covered - accuracy for v6 configurations will be low
Cannot execute commands on actual RouterOS devices - provides guidance only
REST API property names may vary between RouterOS versions
Does not cover third-party tools or unofficial RouterOS modifications

use_cases.title: Network Administrator Automation
use_cases.description: Generate RouterOS CLI commands and REST API calls for configuring firewall rules, IP addresses, interfaces, and system settings across multiple MikroTik devices.

use_cases.title: DevOps Infrastructure as Code
use_cases.description: Create RouterOS scripting language automation for scheduled tasks, monitoring scripts, and configuration backup workflows with proper error handling.

use_cases.title: Technical Support Troubleshooting
use_cases.description: Debug why Linux commands fail on RouterOS, understand architecture-specific package requirements, and navigate device-mode restrictions for feature access.
use_cases.target_user: Support teams diagnosing RouterOS configuration issues

prompt_templates.title: Basic CLI Command Generation
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate RouterOS CLI commands to configure a static IP address 192.168.1.10/24 on ether2 with gateway 192.168.1.1 and DNS servers 8.8.8.8 and 1.1.1.1
prompt_templates.scenario: You need to configure basic network settings on a fresh RouterOS installation

prompt_templates.title: REST API Integration
prompt_templates.prompt: Write TypeScript code using fetch() to connect to RouterOS REST API and list all firewall filter rules that are enabled and have action=drop
prompt_templates.scenario: Building a dashboard to monitor RouterOS firewall configuration

prompt_templates.title: Scripting Automation
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a RouterOS script that checks CPU load every 5 minutes and sends an email alert if average load exceeds 80% for more than 3 consecutive checks
prompt_templates.scenario: Implementing proactive monitoring with automated alerts

prompt_templates.title: Version-Aware Configuration
prompt_templates.prompt: I'm running RouterOS 7.18.2 on an RB5009. Which extra packages do I need for ZeroTier VPN and container support, and what device-mode settings are required?
prompt_templates.scenario: Planning feature installation with device-mode constraints

output_examples.input: Configure a NAT masquerade rule for outgoing traffic on ether1
output: /ip/firewall/nat/add chain=srcnat out-interface=ether1 action=masquerade comment="NAT outgoing traffic"
This command adds a source NAT rule that masquerades all outgoing traffic on ether1. The comment helps identify the rule purpose in the firewall list.

output_examples.input: Check if RouterOS version 7.22 is newer than 7.22rc4
output: Yes, 7.22 (stable release) is newer than 7.22rc4 (release candidate).
Stable releases (no suffix) are considered newer than all rc and beta pre-releases of the same major.minor version.

best_practices: Always verify RouterOS version before applying configurations - syntax and available features vary between versions
Use find expressions with specific property matches to avoid unintended modifications to multiple entries
Test REST API calls with GET (print) operations before executing PUT/PATCH/DELETE mutations
Document device-mode changes and activation timeouts to avoid configuration reverts after reboot
Backup current configuration before running scripts that modify firewall or routing tables
anti_patterns: Do NOT apply Linux shell commands to RouterOS - use RouterOS CLI paths like /ip/address/add instead of ip addr add
Do NOT assume REST API verbs follow standard conventions - PUT creates (add), PATCH updates (set) in RouterOS
Do NOT write scripts without error handling - use :do { ... } on-error={ } blocks for critical operations
Do NOT leave default admin password empty on production devices - change immediately after first login

faq.question: Why don't standard Linux commands work on RouterOS?
faq.answer: RouterOS uses a proprietary system called 'nova' above the Linux kernel. There is no bash shell, no FHS directory structure, and no standard coreutils. All configuration uses RouterOS CLI paths like /ip/address/print or the REST API at /rest/.

faq.question: What is the default password for RouterOS?
faq.answer: Fresh RouterOS installations have username 'admin' with an empty password. Press Enter when prompted for password, then set a secure password immediately. REST API uses Basic auth with 'admin:' (colon with empty string) for initial access.

faq.question: How do I install extra packages like container or zerotier?
faq.answer: Download the .npk package files from download.mikrotik.com for your RouterOS version and architecture. Upload them to the router root directory via SCP or WebFig, then reboot the device. Packages activate on boot.

faq.question: What is device-mode and why can't I enable certain features?
faq.answer: Device-mode (advanced/home/basic/rose) gates access to potentially risky features. Factory default depends on device type. Some features like container require explicit enabling via /system/device-mode/update and physical confirmation (reset button or power cycle).

faq.question: Why does my REST API PUT request create a new entry instead of updating?
faq.answer: RouterOS REST API uses non-standard verb mapping: PUT means 'add' (create), PATCH means 'set' (update). To modify existing entries, use PATCH to /rest/path/*ID where *ID is the internal RouterOS identifier.

faq.question: Does this skill work with RouterOS v6?
faq.answer: No, this skill covers RouterOS v7.x only. v6 has different CLI syntax, no REST API, and different scripting capabilities. Accuracy for v6 configurations will be low - consider upgrading to v7 for API access and modern features.
